planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Chinese fringetree is adaptable to a wide range of soils including heavier clay if drainage is reasonable. It is more heat and drought-tolerant than the native white fringetree, making it an excellent choice for the South.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil in full sun to part shade; Chinese fringetree rivals the native white fringetree in spectacular spring flower display, producing dense, drooping clusters of slender, white, four-petaled flowers that cover the entire tree in a frothy, fragrant cloud of white in May and June, giving the tree the appearance of being draped in snow. The flowers are both fragrant and profuse enough to be visible from a great distance. Male trees tend to produce heavier floral displays than females; female trees produce attractive clusters of dark blue-black berries beloved by birds in late summer and fall. Compared to the native Chionanthus virginicus, the Chinese species blooms slightly later, is notably more heat-tolerant and adaptable in zone 8–9, and has a tidier rounded habit. The large, glossy, leathery leaves are attractive all summer and the gray, furrowed bark is interesting in winter.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in early summer or plant container-grown shrubs in spring; Japanese beautyberry is more compact than American beautyberry (Callicarpa americana) and better suited to smaller gardens. It blooms and fruits reliably even when cut to the ground each spring — a common practice to maintain compact form.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full sun; Japanese beautyberry produces the most vivid violet-purple berries of any ornamental shrub — small, glossy, jewel-like fruits clustered in dense rings along arching stems in late summer and fall, giving a color intensity that photographs cannot fully capture. The effect is stunning both in the landscape and in cut floral arrangements. The foliage turns light yellow in fall before dropping to reveal the berries even more prominently against the bare stems. Unlike American beautyberry (C. americana), which can reach 8–10 feet and become ungainly, Japanese beautyberry stays more compact at 3–6 feet and is better behaved in the border. White-berried ('Leucocarpa') and pink-berried ('Albibaccata') cultivars offer interesting color variations alongside the typical violet-purple.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in late spring or plant container-grown shrubs in spring; cut caryopteris back hard to 4–6 inches in early spring to maintain compact form and maximize flowering — it blooms on new wood produced each season. In zones 5–6 it may die back to the ground but resprouts vigorously from the roots.

directions: Plant in sharply drained, lean, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; blue mist shrub is a compact, late-summer flowering shrub that fills a critical role in the garden: just as most spring and summer shrubs have finished blooming, caryopteris erupts in August through October in dense clusters of small, intense china-blue to violet flowers covering the tops of the aromatic gray-green stems. The blue color is unusually saturated and clean, closer to true blue than almost any other hardy shrub. Bumblebees, honeybees, and other pollinators mob the flowers throughout the extended bloom period. The plant has a pleasant minty-sage fragrance when the foliage is brushed. It thrives in hot, dry, full-sun conditions with excellent drainage and poor soil — conditions that kill many other shrubs. 'Dark Knight' and 'Longwood Blue' are outstanding cultivars with deep blue-violet flowers. Caryopteris is outstanding combined with ornamental grasses and late perennials like sedums and asters.

planting.note: Plant root cuttings (4–6 inch sections of root, placed horizontally 3–4 inches deep) in spring; Russian comfrey 'Bocking 14' is the preferred sterile (non-seeding) cultivar for garden use — it spreads only by root division, so it cannot become invasive the way self-seeding common comfrey can. Once established, it is virtually impossible to eradicate, so site carefully.

directions: Plant root cuttings 3–4 inches deep in moist, fertile soil in sun to part shade; Russian comfrey (Symphytum x uplandicum) is a hybrid between common comfrey and tuberous comfrey, producing a much more vigorous and productive plant than either parent — it is the preferred comfrey for permaculture systems and organic gardening as a dynamic accumulator, deep-rooting perennial that mines subsoil minerals (particularly potassium, phosphorus, calcium, and nitrogen) and concentrates them in its large, hairy leaves that can be cut 3–5 times per year and used as a mulch, liquid fertilizer (composted in water to make comfrey tea), or composted directly. The leaves decompose rapidly and are an outstanding activator for compost piles. The purple-blue flowers are beloved by bumblebees. Unlike common comfrey, 'Bocking 14' does not set viable seed and is non-spreading except through root division, making it the gardener's preferred choice for a controlled, productive stand.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown vines in spring; akebia is semi-evergreen in mild climates and fully deciduous in cold climates. It twines and needs a strong trellis, arbor, or fence. Note: akebia can be invasive in some eastern US states — check local regulations and monitor spread; it naturalizes readily from fallen fruits.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil of average to rich fertility in part shade to full sun; five-leaf akebia is an elegant, twining Asian vine producing delicate, compound leaves with five rounded blue-green leaflets that persist through winter in mild climates, plus unusual, intensely vanilla-chocolate-scented maroon-purple and pale-pink flowers in early spring — the female flowers are larger and darker purple, the male flowers smaller and rose-pink, and both appear together on the same plant before the leaves fully emerge. Two different plants must be present for the interesting, sausage-shaped, gray-purple fruits to develop — edible but bland, with a mild, slightly sweet flesh. The vine is vigorous and tolerates shade better than most flowering vines. In mild climates, akebia can spread aggressively through bird-dispersed seeds and should be monitored; select sterile cultivars or remove fruits if invasiveness is a concern in your region.
